answer:
**In Sheikh Ibrahim Zauq's ghazal "Chupke chupke gham ka khana koi hum se seekh jaaye" (Diwan-E-Zauq, ch. 12), the speaker's secret occupation is feasting on sorrow while hiding the strain.**[^1]

This is the matla (opening couplet), which sets the theme of the entire poem: a series of couplets where the speaker claims mastery ("an art I know") over enduring love's torments in silence, such as weeping inwardly like a cloud, feigning praise while burning with disdain, receiving his own bane gladly, and more.[^2] "Gham ka khana" (eating sorrow) is a poetic metaphor for silently consuming or enduring grief, especially romantic suffering, and the speaker invites others to learn this "art" from him.[^3]

[^1]: Sheikh Ibrahim Zauq, *Diwan-E-Zauq*, ch. 12 ("Chupke chupke gham ka khana koi hum se seekh jaaye"), [paragraph](/works/sheikh-ibrahim-zauq-diwan-e-zauq-0fa798/12-chupke-chupke-gham-ka-khana-koi-hum-se-seekh-jaaye/translation/#p-f1ef29).
[^2]: Sheikh Ibrahim Zauq, *Diwan-E-Zauq*, ch. 12, [full chapter](/works/sheikh-ibrahim-zauq-diwan-e-zauq-0fa798/12-chupke-chupke-gham-ka-khana-koi-hum-se-seekh-jaaye/translation/).
[^3]: Sheikh Ibrahim Zauq, *Diwan-E-Zauq*, ch. 12, [paragraph](/works/sheikh-ibrahim-zauq-diwan-e-zauq-0fa798/12-chupke-chupke-gham-ka-khana-koi-hum-se-seekh-jaaye/translation/#p-f1ef29).
